channel.title: MSc DAAI
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Key Concepts
item.description:
Key Concepts
1. What is marketing?
Why is marketing key to long-term financial performance?
Is marketing worth it?
2. Course method & structure
3. Course evaluation
4. Couse framework
The first principles of marketing
Guiding Questions


What is marketing?
Course method and structure
Course evaluation
Course framework

Definition Marketing is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large.

Large amount of research documents its impact on financial performance, but many people don’t realize the scope of influences on sales and profits.

Grow market size (new products and services, lower prices)
Grow share (better products and services than competition, higher loyalty to retain, and/or steal customers with acquisition strategies)
Better prices and margins (improve loyalty, brand image, relationships, products, targeting of high margin customers)
Reduce costs (WOM, brand, relationships, retain with loyalty


YES!

Improve stock price and reduces cash flow needs.
Marketing capabilities have a larger impact than R&D or operational capabilities since they are “less vulnerable to competitive threat”.
Brands, innovation, and relationships all drive financial performance.
Good marketing leads customers to defend your brand, to act as ambassadors and showing a willingness to do and pay more despite having more affordable options.


6 asynchronous online sessions (videos + readings + test questions)
2 Q&A sessions
Online forum




MCQ tests
The two MCQ tests will be composed of 20 multiple-choice questions each and will take place over Blackboard after having finished the related modules. The first MCQ test is focused on the materials of the intro session, session 1, 2 and 3. The second MCQ test is focused on the materials of session 4, 5 and 6.
These questions will cover all materials indicated by the notion ‘REQUIRED’ on Blackboard. The two tests together represent 40% of the final grade.
See example questions at the end of every session on Blackboard.
For both tests, only 1 attempt can be done!
For the early group (students starting early July), the deadline for completion of the MCQ tests is August 25, 2023 (midnight Paris time).

Individual assignment
The individual assignment is worth 50% of the overall grade. Specific details can be found in the folder ‘Individual Assignment’ under documents. The deadline for completion is September 10, 2023 (midnight Paris time).

Completion of all online modules
10% of the grade is connected to the completion of all online modules and active participation on the Blackboard forums. These criteria will be tracked automatically.


This image outlines a three-phase marketing strategy process:

External & Internal Environment Analysis: Examining the company, consumers, competitors, and external factors (PESTLE analysis). This phase uses SWOT analysis.
Marketing Strategy Planning: Focusing on segmentation, targeting, differentiation, and positioning.
Marketing Mix Development: Developing the 4Ps (Product, Place, Price, Promotion) for the target market.
The process flows from analyzing the environment, to planning the strategy, and finally developing specific marketing tactics.


This image outlines four key principles of marketing strategy and their corresponding marketing actions:

"All customers differ" leads to managing customer heterogeneity.
"All customers change" requires managing customer dynamics.
"All competitors react" necessitates managing sustainable competitive advantage.
"All resources are limited" results in managing resource tradeoffs.
These principles highlight the dynamic and constrained nature of marketing, emphasizing the need for adaptable strategies that account for diverse customer needs, market changes, competitive responses, and resource limitations.


What is marketing?
What are the different stages of the marketing plan?
What are the first principles of marketing?

item.title: Key concepts
item.description:
#market #buyer #market-size #potential-market #market-saturation-rate #market-share #market-penetration #market-growth #market-volatility #pestel #pestel #swot #tows

Key concepts
1. Company
What do we want to know?
Its relation to marketing strategy?
Internal resources and capabilities
2. Consumers and the market
What do we want to know?
Market?
Market attractiveness
1) Market size
Potential market
Market saturation rate
Market share
Market penetration
2) Market and Submarket growth
3) Market volatility
What characterize the buyers?
3. Competition
What do we want to know?
Who is my competitor?
Defining the competitive set
How to delineate the market and thus understand who you are competing with?
Market delineation
4. External Environment
PESTEL
How to write
5. SWOT/TOWS analysis
SW = Internal Factors
OT = External factors
SWOT to TOWS



Company
Consumers
Competition
External environment (PESTEL)
SWOT/TOWS analysis





What are the corporate objectives?
Key strengths and weaknesses?
Definition Corporate strategy entails the overall scope and direction of a firm and the way in which its various business operations work together to achieve specific objectives:
Financial objectives
Customer-oriented objectives
Innovation objectives
Efficiency/internal objectives


This image illustrates the hierarchical structure of marketing strategies within a corporate organization:

At the top is the Corporate Strategy (C), developed by top management at the corporate level.
Below this are Strategic Business Units (SBUs) or markets, labeled as SBU₁, SBU₂, and SBU₃.
Each SBU manages multiple brands. For example, SBU₁ manages brands B₁,₁, B₁,₂, and B₁,₃.
At the bottom level are the marketing instruments, represented by the 4Ps of marketing: Product, Price, Place, and Promotion.
Marketing strategies flow down from the corporate level through SBUs and brands to the specific marketing instruments.
This structure shows how high-level corporate strategy informs and guides more specific marketing decisions at lower levels of the organization, ensuring alignment across different business units and brands.
Info SBUs are responsible for developing overall brand strategy, new P&G product upgrades and innovations, and marketing plans that are reflective of the overall corporate strategy.



What is the company especially good at? What are working points?
What internal capabilities are present to help push the company forward?
What is the financial situation of the company? How much room is created for R&D?
How much money is invested in marketing efforts?





How attractive is the market?
Consumer behavior?

Definition The market is an exchange between 2 (or more) partners, frequently a buyer and a seller. They exchange goods, money, services…etc. #market
Let’s focus on the buyer first.
Definition Buyers entail a group of individuals or organizations having the willingness and ability to buy goods, services…etc to satisfy a particular class of wants or needs. #buyer
Consumers (our focus) → B2C
Organizations → B2B


Market size
Market and submarket growth
Market volatility
Competition (see further)
…

#market-size

Sum of total sales of all firms in the market (i.e. depends on market definition – see later)
4 related metrics:
Potential market
Market saturation rate
Market share
Market penetration rate

#potential-market
The estimated maximum of total sales revenue reachable by all sellers for a good/service in a market during a certain period for a given marketing environment.
Max (how many x how much x how often)
Example:
What is the monthly potential market for toothpaste in France?

French population: 67 mln
% of people that should use toothpaste: 90%
Quantity: 3ml each time
Frequency: 2 times a day (62/month)
PM (Potential Market) month = 67mln x 0,9 x 0,003 x 62 = 11,21 mln liters

The market penetration rate measures how much of the potential market for a product or service has been captured by the actual sales. It is a key indicator of market success and potential growth opportunities.
The formula is:

#market-saturation-rate

#market-share
The percentage of all sales[1] within a market that is held by one brand/product or company.




#market-penetration

Example:
Laundry Care Market – Henkel
Total sales fabric softener = 156 mln EUR
Market potential fabric softener = 276 mln EUR
Total sales of Henkel fabric softener = 60 mln EUR

Penetration rate = 60 / 276 = 21,7%
Market saturation rate = 156 / 276 = 56,5%
Market share = 60 / 156 = 38,5%

Growth is a key criterion to track.
#market-growth

Increased sales may be expected with a stable market share in a growing market. But also more competitors may be attracted…
If market sales decline, competitors may leave that market.
If the aggregate market size declines, there may still be submarkets that grow.




#market-volatility

Market in which sales are very sensitive to economic of seasonal trends to be less attractive than markets in which this is not the case (e.g. sales of soup in B2C market, skiing gear,...)
Sensitivity to economic trends occurs especially with luxury goods & durable consumer goods (lifespan > 3 years).


Needs/wants
Profiling
Nature (B2B vs B2C)
…
Reading: Consumer Behavior and the Buying Process





Who are the (direct) competitors?
What are the strengths/weaknesses?
How does the future look like?
Hans Van Bylen, former CEO Henkel “You have to know your competitor better than you know yourself.”

How “narrow” or “broad” you define your market depends on your customers and how they see the market.
In other words, what do they consider as alternatives to satisfy a specific need/want?

Let’s say a customer thinks “I need a hole in my wall”.
→ What customer needs is a “hole in the wall” (i.e. the need)
Different alternative technologies can satisfy this need.
Technologies are fast changing, while generic needs are rather stable.


What are the needs in a given market?
→ what is being satisfied?
Who are the different “groups of customers” searching for these solutions?
→ who is being satisfied?
What are the alternative technologies available to produce these solutions?
→ how are buyers’ needs satisfied?

Can lead to a narrow or broad delineation of the market…


Broad Market: very often used for a strategic thinking and strategy formulation – thinking long-term and outside the box.
Narrow Market: used for a marketing planning and measuring market share – more practically usable.
Example:
Pepsi Cola Unit

For the competitive, we then want to look at how they perform vis-à-vis our brand.




What are the major trends in society?

Political
Economic
Socio-cultural
Technological
Environment
Legal
#pestel


I.e. here
#pestel

Try to follow a 2-sentence approach:

(Part 1) What is the trend?
Starting as a simple trend, the beard has evolved to the point of becoming a culture in itself. Not ready to give ground, it remains trendy amongst French males who shave less frequently.

(Part 2) How is it relevant for your company? (if you cannot answer this question, the trend is probably less relevant)
This beard trend is significantly hurting sales in the men’s grooming category (-3,3% CAGR ‘13-’18), as the shaving frequency goes down, directly impact Gillette.


#swot






#tows
Goal?
Identify the relationships between S, W, O, T and determine possible strategies based on these combis.

Example:



The sales can be measured by sales value (expressed in currency), sales volume (kg for example) or sales units (the number of units sold)↩︎

item.title: Key concepts
item.description:
#stp #segmentation #targeting #positioning #segmentation #targeting #stp #targeting #ge-matrix

Key concepts
1. Customer Heterogeneity
Advantages of addressing heterogeneity
What causes heterogeneity?
Customer heterogeneity = fundamental to marketing
Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning (STP) approach
2. Segmentation
What criteria can help identify segments?
1) Demographic segmentation
2) Geographic segmentation
3) Psychographic segmentation
4) Behavioral segmentation
The evolution of segmentation
3. Targeting
Segmenation, Targeting, Positioning (STP) approach
Targeting
Moving from segmentation to targeting… (part 1)
Analyzing every segment on different criteria - example:
Ge-matrix
Moving from segmentation to targeting… (part 2)
Reading 1
Reading 2



Customer Heterogeneity
Segmentation
Targeting





Capture diverse needs and wants in the market
Identify unfulfilled needs → market opportunities
Higher customer satisfaction
Reduction of production & promotional costs
Increased competitveness




Every company must address customer heterogeneity when developing an effective marketing strategy
It may be latent or hidden
Customers vary on some underlying preference, but no company is supplying offerings that fit their desires, so those preferences are not evident.
Customers might not even know of their diverse preference, because they have no options to evaluate.
Assuming all customers are the same is a recipe for failure, at least in the long term, as competitors will better satisfy subsegments with more aligned offerings, leading to a downward spiral in which the company has few, less profitable customers that are more costly to serve.
Quote An effective marketing strategy must manage ever-present customer heterogeneity.

#stp

The STP (Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning) model helps in marketing:

Segmentation: Dividing the market into groups with similar needs. #segmentation
Targeting: Selecting the most attractive segments. #targeting
Positioning: Creating a unique image for the product using the marketing mix (Product, Place, Promotion, Price). #positioning
This approach ensures focused and effective marketing strategies.



Definition Segmentation: dividing the market into identifiable groups of consumers (market segment) who share similar set of wants and needs. #segmentation

Consists of dividing the market into groups of customers where:

Customers within a group have very similar needs
Customers across groups have different needs
Suppose this is the market (the buyer side): With segmentation, we want to identify individuals/organizations that have similar needs and wants, and group these together.

Here, we distinguish 3 different needs in the market (blue, yellow, grey). The people with the same need form a segment, which makes that 3 segments exist on this market.

Example: Electric dental care market

General group of consumers, solely interested in basic brushing. This makes we have 6 segments on the toothbrush market with distinct needs homogeneous within the group, heterogenous between groups.


Demographic (who are they?) (who)
Geographic (where do they live?) (who)
Psychographic (how do people think? what do they aspire?) (who)
Behavioral (what are they doing?) (what)
Benefits-sought (what are their needs?) (why)


Gender
Age
Income
Family life-cycle
Family size
Religion
Education
Philosophy? People with similar demographic criteria share (some) similar needs and wants.


Division of the market based on the location of the customers.
Philosophy? People who live in the same area share (some) similar needs and wants that are different from people who are living in the other parts of the world. (Really?)
Example: McDonald’s launched local burgers across the world to meet the needs of people living within specific areas.


Philosophy? People with similar psychographics share (some) similar needs and wants (Really??)


Users vs non-users vs ex-users
First-time users vs regular users
Heavy, medium, light users
…
Philosophy? People who behave similarly share (some) similar needs
and wants. (Really??)
How strongly do demographic, geographic psychographic and behavioral criteria correlate with needs and wants??
→ Sometimes strongly… sometimes not…
Stereotypes. ### 5) Benefits-sought segmentation
The benefits-sought is the only true way to discern segments, as we really use the WHY consumers make decisions to divide the market and this WHY essentially forms the basis of heterogeneity on the market.

Segmentation based on specific needs/desires on the market
Considers the various responses to the question “What is desired in an offering?”

The core goal of segmentation is to identify groups of customer who have similar needs, desires, and subsequent behaviors. Hence, true customer segments are groups of customers with similar preferences (needs, desires, behaviors). This makes a benefits-sought approach the core way to segment the market.
The other variables – demographics, geographics, psychographics, behaviors – then help in a later stage for:

Profiling customer segments and,
predicting segment membership as they are easily observable criteria.
Segments often get a “persona” = PROFILING

Once the segmentation is done, companies seek to better understand WHO is part of the segment (e.g., gender monetary value, etc).
Profiling of the various segments is done.
Today, this is often labeled as creating diverse “personas”.
Profiling happens using the variable pool that may also serve for segmentation.
Develop a profile of every segment - example: Elite Athletes:








Very smart move from Coca-Cola. They can collect a large amounts of data (consumer behaviors, needs and wants) through this program.


#targeting


#stp

Dividing the market into identifiable groups of consumers (market segment) who share a similar set of wants and needs.
Think back of our 3 segments – yellow, blue and grey… Targeting involves choosing which segment(s) we want to be serving.

Suppose we consider the yellow and grey segment to be a good fit with our company, and the blue segment not… Our targeting step may imply that we select the yellow and grey segment to serve.


#targeting
After segmenting the market, a company needs to select 1 or more segments to target (i.e., segments a company decides to serve)
How?

Evaluate segment attractiveness
segment size
growth potential
profitability potential
Evaluate competition
competitor strengths
competitor intensity
competitor resources
Evaluate company fit
corporate objectives
competencies
resources
An ideal target segment meets six criteria:

Based on customer needs (segments stands by itself)
Different from other segments (little crossover competition)
Differences match company’s competences (able to execute within resource constraints)
Sustainable (can keep customers)
Customers are identifiable (can find customers)
Financially valuable (can generate sufficient revenue)





A GE-matrix can be used to support the targeting process
Info The GE matrix, also known as the GE/McKinsey Matrix, is a strategic tool used for multi-business corporations to prioritize their investment among various business units. Developed by General Electric with the help of McKinsey & Company, it evaluates business units on two axes: industry attractiveness and business unit strength. Each axis is divided into three levels (high, medium, low), creating a 3x3 grid that results in nine cells, each representing a different strategic implication ranging from investment and growth to selective investment or divestiture.
Definition A GE-matrix : visual tool to plot segment attractiveness based on market attractiveness and firm’s competitive strength to approach a segment. #ge-matrix

The size of each “bubble” indicates the size of the market segment. Gym Socialites or Fashion Trend Setters are the “best” segments for this firm as they are in the upper right corner of the matrix and are larger markets.


In some, to decide whether you wanted to develop a value proposition for a segment, you basically have to estimate three different things:

The attractiveness of that segment,
The competitiveness levels of that segment,
Whether you want and you can make a difference as a company in addressing this segment.
item.guid: Marketing managment/Session 2 - Segmentation and Targeting/Segmentation Targeting.md

item.title: Key concepts
item.description:
#positioning #usp #customer-value-proposition #nps #positioning-statement #usp #perceptual-maps #repositioning

Key concepts
1. Positioning
Customer Value Proposition
Positioning Statement
Unique Selling Proposition (USP)
Quick recap
The value of perceptual maps
2. Re-positioning
The case of Axe (Lynx)
Re-positioning
3. Sustainable Competitive Advantage
Success = attraction of competition
Offering Equity
Brand Equity
Relationship Equity



Positioning
Re-positioning
Sustainable Competitive Advantage (SCA)
Offering equity
Brand equity
Relationship equity



Definition Positioning: process of improving your relative advantage in the minds of your targeted customers. #positioning

This diagram explains brand positioning by showing its connection to consumers and the company.
Brand positioning involves designing a company’s offering and brand image to occupy a distinctive place in the target market’s mind. This helps clarify the differences of the offering and makes the brand easier to remember for consumers.
For the company, it provides guidelines for developing marketing strategies and ensures coherence in the marketing mix.
The ultimate goal is to maximize the brand’s potential benefit to the firm by locating it effectively in consumers’ minds.

Customer Value Proposition: a strategic tool facilitating communication of an organisation’s ability to share resources and offer a superior value package to targeted customers.
Positioning Statement: distillation of the brand’s value proposition into a compelling answer to the all-important question: “Why should I buy?”.
Unique Selling Proposition (USP): Value claim that offers a prospective customer a specific, unique, and superior reason to purchase a product. For external purposes. #usp

#customer-value-proposition


Customer essentially buy “value” in a trade-off with “price”
Multiple value elements exist at 4 different levels
The more elements companies deliver, the higher their loyalty and revenues
Different combinations are more important for different industries
https://www.bain.com/insights/elements-of-value-interactive/

Companies exceeding on 4 or more elements have, on average, 3 times the NPS (Net Promoter Score) of companies strong on 1 element and 20 times the NPS of companies strong on 0 element. #nps
You cannot excel at all the 30 value elements (e.g., Apple excels at 11 elements)
You need to chose elements strategically!

Positioning Statement For [Target_Market], [Brand_X] is the only brand among all [Competitve_Set], that [Unique_Value_claim] because [Reason_to_Believe]. #positioning-statement
Positioning statements distill the brand’s value proposition into a compelling answer to the all-important question: “Why should I buy?”.

For whom, for when, for where?
→ Target market segment: mothers concerned about their child’s health; when you celebrate your birthday
What value?
→ Types of value: economic, functional, experimental, and/or social
Why & how?
→ Reasons to believe: logical arguments, scientific and technological data, consumer testimonials, celebrity or expert endorsements…
Relative to whom?
→ Competitive set
Example: Ethos water bottle
For [consumers who are socially conscious], [Ethos] is the only brand among all [bottled waters] that [cares about solving the world’s clean water crisis], because [it donates five cents for every bottle sold to support humanitarian programs in coffee-growing communities, providing clean, safe water to those in need].

#usp

USP is a value claim that offers a (prospective) customer a specific, unique and superior reason to purchase a product.
It typically hinges on a specific benefit/value element that competitors cannot copy easily and that is resonant and relevant enough to persuade consumers to buy.

Examples:
Rational:

Dominos: “you get fresh, hot pizza delivered to your door in 30 minutes or less or it’s free”
Head&shoulders: “effectively targets the source of dandruff”
Emotional:

Haribo: “makes children and adults happy”
Red Bull: “gives you wings”



#perceptual-maps

Map of where brands (or product versions) are perceived in consumers’ minds.
What brands are closest competitors?
Suggests possible market opportunities

Reading: Building Perceptual Maps


#repositioning
Sometimes, your positioning is no longer profitable or outdated…


Original positioning: “Hyper masculinity” – Attraction = conquest.


Axe realized its positioning was not future-proof

Why? changing (commercial) environment & societal norms
Criticism: objection of women ≠ inconsistent with cultural movements
Outcome:
gradual loss of connection with existing customers
lower relevance for future prospects
Quote ”In the years preceding the campaign, it was clear that women didn’t like the way gender was represented," he adds. "If you are selling attraction to men, you need women involved too. To have deeper rates of engagement with women, Axe needed to change.”
Danny Feeney (72and Sunny)
→ Use of qualitative research – consumer consulting board.
Insights:

Individuality is inspirational – brands that encourage consumers to be themselves – as opposed to conforming – will thrive.
50% of men said they were afraid of looking different for fear of being judged.
98% of men said they had been told to “man up” at some point in their life.
90% of women said they like men who are “true to themselves”
New positioning: Individuality & Being oneself – Attraction = a connection
The goal? Liberating men from a “toxic” version of masculinity



39.3 milion digital views
Positive sentiment: +27%
4 bilion media impressions
Purchase consideration: +4%

Repositioning = hard & difficult process:

Ensure relevance of the positioning – e.g., fit with the good/service
Secure customers’ permission for the positioning
existing customers → avoid the positioning to damage self-image/identity (i.e., need for congruence)
prospects needs to believe the brand
Deliver the new positioning = ADAPT the entire marketing mix





The more successful a firm is, as reflected in its sales, profits, and stock prices, the more effort its competitors expend to attack its financially successful position.
We need to think how we can remain successful in the long run through developing our marketing mix in such a way that we help build customer equity.
From 1929 to 2013, the Dow Jones top firms were replaced 56 times due to bankruptcy, poor performance, or other reasons, reflecting their failures to respond successfully to market changes and competitive threats.

Need to develop a sustainable competitive advantage…

Offering Equity: how much customers would pay an unlabeled glass of beer (e.g., blind taste test).
Brand Equity: the difference between the price a customer would pay for an unlabeled glass of beer and the same beer after learning the brand.
Relationship Equity: the difference between the price a customer would pay for a glass of branded beer and having the same beer served by his or her favorite bartender.


→ Continuously invest in quality & innovation

New offerings often motivate customers to switch from competitors to the innovative firm, to gain access to the new product
New offerings also can help the firm acquire new customers or enter new markets when they offer similar performance but at a lower price
Offering new and innovative products tends to enhance the firm’s brand, even if customers don’t buy the new offering


Brands can be highly valuable and make that customers are willing to pay extra, develop strong emotional attachment…
Brand equity lies in the mind of the customer → which makes it hard to copy and to change!


Many companies invest significantly in the building and maintaining of strong relationships with their customers.
Efforts to build relationship equity often come after generating brand and value equities, because relational interactions are part of the good and service delivery or experience, which occurs after the product is designed and launched and brands have been promoted to the targeted customer segment.
Relationship equity is driven by feelings of gratitude, reciprocity, trust and commitment.

Typical tactic: community building
Typical tactic: loyalty programs

item.title: Table of content
item.description:
#product #packaging #brand #brand-architecture #product-mix


Table of content
Key Concepts
1. Product
Product?
Different product types
What “core” decisions need to be made?
2. Product design & features
Design & Features
- Avoid complexity
- Barriers to adoption
Research & Support are critical!
3. Packaging
Packaging
Details matter
Be mindful about changes
4. Brand name and extensions
Quick reminder
Brand Architecture
Extension Strategies
Brand Extension Strategies
5. Portfolio management
1) Optimization
2) Innovation
6. Connected Strategies
Types of Connected Strategies
1) Respond-to-Desire
2) Curated Offering
3) Coaching Behavior
4) Automated Execution
Key Capabilities for Connected Strategies
Example:
Conclusion:



Product
Product design & features
Packaging
Brand and line extensions
Portfolio management
Connected Strategies




Definition Product: Anything that satisfies a want or a need through use, consumption, or acquisition. #product

The product should reflect the organisation’s value proposition
Can be about goods or services, or any combination.
Today, most brands offer a mix of goods and services… in combination, they deliver solutions to customers.

Convenience products:
Products that consumer buy frequently & immediately

Low priced
Many purchase locations
includes: staple goods, impulse goods, emergency goods
Shopping products:
Products that consumer buy less frequently

Gather product information
Fewer purchase locations
Speciality products:
Products that consumers are willing to make a special purchasing effort

Unique characteristics
Brand identification
Fewer purchase locations
Unsought products:
Products that consumers don’t (like to) think about, don’t realize they may be useful,…


Features & design: how does the product look like?
Packaging: how do we wrap the product?
Brand name: what name do we give the product?



3 core dimensions:

Functionality – what is the product supposed to do?
functionality
utility (i.e., performance)
technology factors (i.e., ingredients, components, hardware, software…)
Aesthetics – perception of beauty, appearance/form, sensorial appeal
visual appeal
appeal to other senses (smell, taste,…)
Symbolism – perceived message a product communicates (to oneself or others)
resonance with self-image, personality, values
helpfulness in communicating status, gender, social roles
communication of group membership


Avoid too many features → make products overwhelming for consumers.
Leads to excessive product returns, lower repurchase intentions and negative word-of-mouth.
Pur User Experience (UX) central in product development.



Perceived complexity
Perceived security dependence
Perceived health risk
Technology anxiety
Perceived benefit
Habitual buying behavior
…






#packaging
Opportunity to communicate with the consumer.
Provision of:

(product category) information
Positioning of a product – brand identity & values
Attracting attention
Relationship building
Decisions are necessary to:

Graphics: color, picture, typeface,…
Structure: shape,…
Material: plastic, glass, paperboard,…
Trade-off between customer attractiveness & handling viability (cost of manufacturing, transportation, etc…)

Machine-written typeface (e.g., Helvetica, Futura) vs Hand-written typeface (e.g., Moon Flower, DJB This Is Me).
Hand-written fonts may positively affect purchase behaviors in case of hedonic products not well-known to a consumer (through perceptions of human presence).
Machine-written fonts tend to go better with functional products as they are perceived as technical and professional.



In this case, many consumers didn’t recognize the product on supermarket shelves. Some loyal consumers saw the “100% Orange Juice” and asked themselves if the product was still the same as the Tropical Pure Premium they always trusted. Then appeared a series of confusions in consumers’ minds who lost their main reference elements to recognize the product.




Brand? A name, term, symbol, or any other feature that identifies one seller’s good or service as distinct from those of other sellers. #brand
Brand elements: name (e.g., Apple), symbol (e.g., silhouette of an apple with a bite removed), package design (e.g., sleek white box),…
Brands embody a core promise of values and benefits consistently delivered.

Definition Brand architecture: defines both the rationale and the structure among the firm, its products, and its brand/product extensions – in essence, how the brand is used at different levels in the organisation. #brand-architecture

House of Brands:

New products are assigned new names with no obvious connection to existing brands offered by the company
The marketing organisation must work hard to establish each of the brand in the market
It allows brands to stand on their own thus lessening threats that may occur to other brands marketed by the company
Each brand builds its own separate equity which allows the company, if they choose, to sell off individual brands without impacting other brands owned by the company
Branded House:

New products are placed under the umbrella of an existing brand
It enables the organisation to rapidly build market awareness and acceptance
The potential disadvantage is that the market has already established certain perceptions of the brand. For instance, a company that sells low-end, lower priced products may have a brand that is viewed as an economy brand. The brand image may create customer confusion and hinder the company if they attempt to introduce higher-end, higher priced products using the same brand name.

Line extension: parent brand is used to brand a new product within a same product category
Brand extension: parent brand is used to enter a different category


Successful brand extensions occurs when the parent brand is seen as having:

Favorable associations
Perception of fit between the parent brand and the extension product
perceived brand “typically” i.e., product categories which “go well” with the parent brand
emotional agreement with the potential extension product categories




Brands need to consider how they can optimize existing products in their portfolio (further penetration of existing markets – or enter new markets).
Brands need to consider how to renew and further innovate (new product varieties and new products overall).


A product mix is the set of all products and items a particular seller offers for sale. #product-mix
Product Mix Considerations:

Width: Number of product lines
Length: Total number of items in a line
Depth: Number of versions of each product
Consistency: Distance between product lines in end use

Apple’s product mix is strategically managed across its various product lines (Mac, iPad, iPhone, etc.).
Product Mix of Apple:

Width: Apple has 7 main product lines – Mac, iPad, iPhone, Apple Watch, Music, Apple TV, and HomePod.
Length: Each product line contains multiple items, such as various models of MacBooks, iPads, iPhones, Apple Watches, and other devices.
Depth: Within each product line, there are different versions of each product (e.g., different storage capacities, colors, and connectivity options).
Consistency: Apple’s product lines are highly consistent in terms of quality, design, and ecosystem integration, focusing on enhancing the user’s digital experience across computing, communication, entertainment, and smart home functionality.
Optimization Checklist:

Fit with customer needs?
Fit to strategy/business model?
Cannibalization risk?
Cost of production? Diversification comes at a cost…
Competitors' product mix?


Innovation is difficult
Success rate = low
Consider cross-industry variation








Quick and seamless fulfillment of customer requests


What? Providing customers with services and goods that they’ve requested – and doing so as quickly and seamlessly as possible.
Essential capabilities? fast delivery, minimal friction, flexibility, precise execution
Examples:

Uber & Lyft: cars arrive promptly, and info is given
Amazon “once click” purchase process & Alexa orders

Personalized recommendations based on customer data


What? Getting actively involved in helping customers at an earlier stage of the customer journey – after customers have figured out what they need, but before they’ve decided how to fill this need. Essentially, steering customers toward goods and services the company can easily provide.
Essential capabilities? personalized recommendation process
Examples:

Netflix content suggestions
Hello Fresh personally tailored offerings

Proactive reminders and encouragement to achieve goals


What? Proactively reminding customers of their needs and encouraging them to take steps to achieve their goals → “parental role”
Essential capabilities? develop a deep understanding of customer needs (i.e., what does the customer really want to achieve?), ability to gather and interpret contextual data.
Examples:

Nike+ : virtual running clubs, audio training guides,…
La Roche Posay: My Skin Track UV

Anticipating and fulfilling needs without customer input
BEFORE you realize that you WILL run out of toner…

The doorbell rings and you are surprised to see FedEx delivering a box.
You don’t recall having ordered anything
You unpack the box and find a set of toner cartridges for your printer. Odd.
You walk back to your office and start printing your letter.
Your computer alerts you hat your printer is about to run out of toner!


What? Customers authorize a company to take care of something, and from that point the company handles everything.
Essential capabilities? strong trust, a rich flow of information from the customer, ability to use data to anticipate needs.
Examples:

HP Instant Ink
Tesla over-the-air software updates


Fast delivery and minimal friction
Personalization and recommendation algorithms
Deep understanding of customer needs
Data gathering and interpretation
Building trust and managing rich information flows



Disney's MyMagic+ system demonstrates connected strategies in action:

Wristbands personalize experiences, manage traffic flow, store Fast Passes, and more.
The system collects data to understand customer behavior and optimize offerings.
Disney provides it guests with an RFID driven bracelet that captures personal information of guests and allows tracking them. And imagine the person in the Woody suit can connect to these bracelets and gets information on who a guest is what their name is. And when he comes up to you randomly and says, “hey Arna, how are you doing today?”.


In today's connected world, product management is about more than just developing and selling individual items. It’s about creating integrated solutions, anticipating needs, and building lasting relationships with customers through technology and data.

item.title: Table of contents
item.description:
#place #channel-structure #retailing #producer #retailer #planogram #perceptual-maps #promotion #6-M #market #mission #targeting #customer-value-proposition #usp #positioning-statement #impression #GRP #CPM #CTR #cpc


Table of contents
Key concepts
1. Place
Place?
Some key decisions?
Delivery Model
Channel Structure
Examples
What factors impact the length of the channel structure?
Retail Partner Selection
Producer Considerations
Retailer Considerations
Producer-Retailer conflicts may happen…
In-store place decisions
Planogram
Special Places in Store
Placement Fees
New Platforms
Omnichannel Retailing
The power of platform models
Platforms models
2. Promotion
The Integrated Marketing Communications Plan
Market
Mission
Message
1) Message content
2) Message Structure & Format
Humor appeal:
Sex appeal
Fear appeal
3) Message source
Celebrities
Influencers
Who to adopt as influencer for your campaign?
Media
Money
Measurement
Advertising Metrics
1. Impressions (exposures – opportunities-to-see (OTS))
2. Gross Rating Points (GRPs)
3. Cost per Thousand Impressions (CPM)
4. Net Reach
5. Average Frequency
6. Effective Reach
7. Clickthrough Rate (CTR)
8. Cost per Click (CPC)


Place:

Delivery model
Retail partner selection
In-store place decisions
New platforms
Promotion:

6-M model
Designing messages
Influencer marketing
Madia channels




Definition Place: the process of moving products from the producer to the intended user… in other words, it is how a product is bought and where it is bought #place





Delivery model
Retail partner selection
In-store place decisions


Definition Channel Structure: Refers to the path a product takes from producer to consumer. #channel-structure

Different delivery models exist in every industry
Range from short (i.e., direct) to long
Different types may be combined (i.e., multichannel approach)

Level 0: Direct from producer to customer (e.g., online stores, direct mail).
Level 1: Producer to retailer/platform to customer (e.g., department stores, online marketplaces).
Level 2: Producer to wholesaler/distributor to retailer to customer (e.g., FMCG products).
Level 3: Producer to agent/broker to wholesaler/distributor to retailer to customer (e.g., complex products).



Level 0 (Direct Channel): Apple selling MacBooks directly to customers via its website.
Level 1 (One-Level Channel): Apple selling iPhones through retailers like FNAC and Amazon to customers.
Level 2 (Two-Level Channel): P&G products distributed by McKesson to retailers like Remedy’sRx, then to customers.
Level 4 (Agent/Broker Channel):
Example: Van der Does Spice Brokers:

Structure: Manufacturer → Agent/Broker → Wholesaler/Distributor → Retailer → Customers
Description: Van der Does Spice Brokers acts as an intermediary, adding value to the supply chain by providing services like auditing, negotiation, and contract management.


In what circumstances is longer “better”?
In what circumstances is shorter “better”?


Producer Factors: Producers with adequate resources, broad product lines, and a need for channel control may prefer shorter channels.
Competitive Factors: Manufacturers satisfied with the performance of their marketing intermediaries may opt for longer channels.

Definition Retailing: includes all the activities involved in selling goods or services directly to final consumers for their personal, non-business use. #retailing
Various formats (online & offline):

speciality stores (i.e., Decathlon)
supermarkets (i.e., Intermarché)
discount stores (i.e., Action)
superstores/hypermarkets (i.e., Auchan)
department stores (i.e., Galeries Lafayette)
convenience stores (i.e., Spar)
off-price retailers (i.e., GAP)
online retailers/marketplaces (i.e., Amazon)


#producer
Product Availability:

Distribution intensity (intensive, selective, exclusive)
In-store positioning (shelf-facings, occupation rate)
Promotional Ability:

Retailer's ability to execute point-of-purchase promotions
Collaborative campaigns.
Cost-Effectiveness:

Transportation cost
Inventory costs
Middlemen margins
Retailer investments.
Customer Considerations: Retailer image, target customer shopping habits, and brand equity alignment.

Where do customer shop?
What criteria do they find important for (online) store choice? Level of brand equity?


#retailer
Revenue Potential:





Investment Requirements:

Investment needed to support the product intro
Investments in producer relationship: IT systems, delivery systems,…
Investments provided by the producer: POS-investment (Point of Sale),…
Assortment Competition:

Fit with existing product assortment
Competition with private labels
Producer/Brand Equity

Channel conflict exists when channel members interfere with each others’ objectives:

Horizontal conflict involves firms on same level – e.g., grocery store vs drug store
Vertical conflict involves firms at different levels – e.g., producer vs wholesaler, producer vs retailer
Channel power is the ability to influence or determine behavior of others in channel (based on expertise, rewards, sanctions, size of business…)

This is an objective strongly linked to the job of category managers
→ i.e., people tasked to grow the overall revenue generated for a specific category for a retailer; work both at the retail and producer side.
Key decision 1: in what aisle do we put the product(s)?
Key decision 2: Where in the aisle do we put the product(s)?


Definition It is a visual representation providing details as to where a product should be placed on a shelf and how many facings a product should have. Tool used by retailers and supplier alike. #planogram #perceptual-maps
Retailer goal: optimize shelf space in order to increase sales.
Supplier goal: justify space allocation to brands and help new product development.

Special product placements can enhance visibility and sales:

End-cap Displays: Located at the end of aisles to attract attention.
Check-out Placement: Positioned near the register for impulse purchases.
Entry Aisle Promotion Zones: Placed at the store entrance to catch customers' attention as they enter.




The fees a food manufacturer pays for placement of its product anywhere in a store, wether by the register, on a endcap (an end-of-aisle display), by the deli, or on a shelf.
Display fees: payments for premium spots like an endcap, cardboard displays that retailers call “shippers”, or the “shelf talker” signs on shelf facings that call attention to a product.
Pay-to-Stay fees: payments manufacturers must make to remain on a store’s shelf. These are not necessarily cash payments; often manufacturers compensate retailers in the form of free merchandise and reduced prices.
Slotting fees[1]: the fees manufacturers pay to introduce a new product on a store’s shelf.



Omnichannel retailing integrates multiple channels (online and offline) to enhance customer experience and sales, creating synergies rather than cannibalizing channels.


Types of Platform Business Models:

Social Media Platforms: Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter
Communication Platforms: WhatsApp, Skype, WeChat, LINE
Search Platforms: Google, Bing, Yahoo, Safari
Sharing Economy Platforms: Airbnb, Uber, ZipCar
Peer-to-peer sharing
Platform owner-provided assets
Capacity-unconstrained resources (e.g., file sharing)
Matching Platforms: TaskRabbit, Tinder, e-Harmony
Content and Review Platforms: YouTube, TripAdvisor, Yelp
Booking Platforms: Booking.com, Expedia, Pagoda
Retail Platforms: Amazon, Etsy, eBay, Craigslist
Payment Platforms: PayPal, Alipay, Visa
Development Platforms: App stores, gaming consoles
Crowdsourcing and Crowdfunding Platforms: InnoCentive, Kickstarter



Quadrant 1:
Description: Marketer-provided resources with a transfer of ownership.
Example: Traditional retail platforms where products are sold directly to consumers.
Quadrant 2:
Description: Peer-to-peer-provided resources with a transfer of ownership.
Example: Marketplaces like eBay, where individuals sell their own goods to other individuals.
Quadrant 3:
Description: Marketer-provided resources with access-based use; no transfer of ownership.
Example: Subscription services like Netflix, where content is provided by the company but only accessed by users.
Quadrant 4:
Description: Peer-to-peer-provided resources with access-based use; no transfer of ownership.
Example: Sharing economy platforms like Airbnb, where individuals rent out their properties to others without transferring ownership




Definition Promotion involves using communication to foster customer engagement behaviors such as buying or word-of-mouth. It includes a wide range of activities aimed at influencing the target audience's perceptions and actions. #promotion
Plethora of online/offline communication/promotion channels exists



#6-M

Market
Mission
Message
Media
Money
Measurement

Effective communication strategies depend on understanding the target audience. Identifying and selecting customer segments is crucial for tailoring messages and choosing the right media. #market
Check 1: Who do you want to reach? (i.e., target segment(s))
Check 2: Where can you find these people (i.e., based on consumer profiling).


#mission
The mission defines the specific goals of the communication campaign.
Goals:

Attracting new customers
Winning back lost customers
Encouraging trials
Showcasing product/service use
Encouraging brand switching
Creating a brand image
Reminding customers
Highlighting brand position
Stimulating extra sales
Lowering the cost of sales
Enhancing brand loyalty
Reducing cognitive dissonance
Goal Statement:

selected target audience #targeting
definition of impact on audience
timeline of impact
degree of change required
Example Statement: increase awarness of the 3-year warranty on all of our products to 50% of the population by June 1, 2019.


Key Decisions for Message Design

Message Content: What message to deliver based on communication goals.
Message Structure & Format: How to structure and format the message to ensure clarity and engagement.
Message Source: Who delivers the message (e.g., experts, celebrities, influencers, typical consumers).


What message do you want to deliver?
Communication goals are the key driver of the message content

#customer-value-proposition #usp #positioning-statement

Authentic brand activism = key!
901,000 dislikes on Youtube vs 468,000 likes in the first few weeks after release. Raising questions about virtue signaling…

✅ Fit with the brand (history)?
✅ Fit with brand audience?
✅ Brand authenticity
✅ Long-term commitment

How to structure and format the message (informational vs. transformational appeal, use of humor, sex appeal, fear appeal).
Informational Appeal:

Gives a reason to buy that is practical or functional
e.g., product quality, economic value, performance claims, technical attributes
Transformational Appeal:

Tries to stir up positive or negative emotions to motivate engagement
e.g., fear, guilt, sex, shame, love, humor, pride, joy…


Attracts attention
Entertains customers
May enhance word-of-mouth
Yet, NO clear impact on purchase behavior


Sex appeals in advertising can be found in:

Physical features of models (clothing, physique and general attractiveness)
Behavior/movement
Intimacy between models
Contextual features
Gratuitous use of sex in advertising:

Ads with sexual appeal are remembered more than ads without it (due to higher attention and message processing, which facilitates message retrieval)
BUT no significant positive/negative impact on the brand itself!
No extra impact on purchase intentions compared to ads without sexual appeal.
An unapropriate fit (e.g., sexual appeal for a tool set, USB drive, breakfast cereal) leads to negative outcomes.
Sexual appeal works better for men than it does for women -> higher ad liking.


Fear appeals are successful at influencing:

attitudes
intentions
behaviors
They work better for women than men and they work better for one-time (e.g., getting vaccinated) versus repeated behaviors (e.g., exercising multiple times per week every week)


Who delivers the message (expert, celebrity, influencer, typical consumer).
Some checks:

Expertise: does the communication possess (specialized) knowledge to back the claim?
Trustworthiness: how objective and honest is the source perceived to be?
Likability: how attractive is the source?
Typical choice:

Expert (e.g., doctor dentist…)
Attractive spokesperson (celebrity)
Influencer (online – social media)
“Typical” consumer


Celebrity endorsements can positively impact attitudes & behaviors under certain conditions.

Celebrity – product match is necessary (e.g., attractive actor/actress – perfume)
Male endorses > female endorsers
Implicit endorsements > explicit endorsements
Actors > models, musicians, TV hosts
Endorsements of unfamiliar object > familiar object
Quality seals, awards > celebrity endorsement



Celebrity influencer: brand awareness
Mega influencer: brand awareness
Macro influencer: product consideration, purchase intent
Micro influencer: product consideration, purchase intent
Nano influencer: word-of-mouth and engagement within a specific niche

Influencers can significantly boost brand awareness, product consideration, and engagement. Key considerations for influencer marketing include:

Relevance of the influencer – i.e., fit with your product? fit with your audience?
Reach of the influencer – i.e., how much people can you reach?
Resonance of the influencer – i.e., what is the level of engagement that can be created?
What is the ‘trust-level’ of the influencer - i.e., how well is the influencer perceived by their audience?
What is the sponsorship saturation of the influencer? – i.e. how often does the influencer promote products? which ones?
What budget is involved? What is needed to bring the influencer on board?
How much creative freedom do you want to give to the influencer?
Transparency of the influencer? – i.e., openness on being sponsored? (#ad, #sponsored)




Selecting the right media channels is crucial for reaching the target audience effectively. The Media Trifecta includes:

Paid Media: Advertising through paid channels (e.g., TV ads, social media ads).
Owned Media: Channels controlled by the brand (e.g., website, social media pages).
Earned Media: Publicity gained through word-of-mouth or organic mentions (e.g., reviews, shares).
Channel Selection:

Consider target audience media consumption.
Align media choices with campaign goals.
Evaluate affordability and cost-effectiveness of each channel.
Digital Advertising: A rapidly growing segment of the advertising market, encompassing search advertising, social media advertising, banner advertising, video advertising, and classifieds.





Checklist:
✅ What media is my target population frequenting?
✅ What media fit my campaign goal(s) best?
✅ What media can I afford and is cost-effective?

A budget needs to be determined
Fixed percentage of sales

This approach assumes that sales are directly related to advertising
If the rule is applied when sales are declining…?
Task-based

Setting marketing objectives based on the “tasks” that the advertising has to complete
Residual-based

To base the advertising budget on what the business can afford – after all other expenditure
Same level as competitors (→ see benchmark reports)

When products are well-established with predictable sales patterns
It could also prevent a business with competitive advantages from increasing market share by spending more than average

Planning is crucial!

Our estimates on time-of-day effects suggest that, ceteris paribus, posting stories in the morning generates approximately an 8.8% (11.1%) increase in link clicks compared to posting stories in the afternoon (evening). Assuming that page depth is 1 and the cost per impression is $0.06 (the lowest observed return among the seven categories), this 8.8% (11.1%) increase translates into a gross profit of $25,529 ($32,201) for a content platform that posts 5,000 free stories per year.
Theory? Higher availability of working memory in the morning → higher levels of alertness, curiosity, and information seeking.
As the day continues → stress increases → working memory availability diminishes → less desire to engage with social media content.


Evaluating the success of a promotional campaign requires specific metrics.




Purpose: Understand how many times an ad is viewed
#impression



Purpose: Measure impressions in relation to the number of people in the audience
#GRP



Purpose: Measure cost-effectiveness of impression generation
#CPM



Purpose: Measure breadth of an ad campaign



Purpose: Measure how strongly an ad is concentrated on a given population



Purpose: Measure portion of an audience that is exposed to an ad enough times to be influenced
*A frequency of 3 is considered as a rule of thumb



Purpose: Measure effectiveness of a web ad by counting how many are intrigued to click on it
#CTR



Purpose: Measure or establish the cost-effectiveness of advertising
#cpc



Note: some people use “slotting fee” more generically to refer to any payment a manufacturer makes for space inside a store.↩︎
item.guid: Marketing managment/Session 5 - Place and Promotion/Place and Promotion.md

item.title: Marketing Management: Place and Promotion
item.description:
Marketing Management: Place and Promotion
(1) Place
(1.1) Place – Delivery Model
(1.2) Place – Retail Partner Selection
(1.3) Place – In-store place decisions
(1.4) Place – New Platforms
(2) Promotion
(2.1) Promotion - Market
(2.2) Promotion - Mission
(2.3) Promotion - Message
(2.4) Promotion - Media
(2.5) Promotion - Money
(2.6) Promotion - Measurement



Definition: Place refers to the process of moving products from the producer to the intended user. It encompasses how a product is bought and where it is bought.
Key elements:
Delivery model
Retail partner selection
In-store placement decisions
New platforms
Trend: The growth of e-commerce is significantly impacting "place" decisions, with online sales steadily increasing globally.


Channel Structure: Refers to the path a product takes from producer to consumer.
Level 0: Direct from producer to customer (e.g., online stores, direct mail).
Level 1: Producer to retailer/platform to customer (e.g., department stores, online marketplaces).
Level 2: Producer to wholesaler/distributor to retailer to customer (e.g., FMCG products).
Level 3: Producer to agent/broker to wholesaler/distributor to retailer to customer (e.g., complex products).
Channel Length:
Short channels are suitable for business users, geographically concentrated markets, products requiring technical knowledge and servicing, large orders, perishable or complex, and expensive products.
Long channels are suitable for consumers, geographically diverse markets, products with low technical knowledge and servicing requirements, small orders, durable and standardized, and relatively inexpensive products.
Producer Factors: Producers with adequate resources, broad product lines, and a need for channel control may prefer shorter channels.
Competitive Factors: Manufacturers satisfied with the performance of their marketing intermediaries may opt for longer channels.


Retailing: Encompasses all activities involved in selling goods or services directly to final consumers for personal use.
Retail Formats: Specialty stores, department stores, supermarkets, convenience stores, discount stores, superstores/hypermarkets, off-price retailers, and online retailers/marketplaces.
Producer Considerations:
Product Availability: Distribution intensity (intensive, selective, exclusive) and in-store positioning (shelf-facings, occupation rate)
Promotional Ability: Retailer's ability to execute point-of-purchase promotions and collaborative campaigns.
Cost-Effectiveness: Transportation cost, inventory costs, middlemen margins, and retailer investments.
Customer Considerations: Retailer image, target customer shopping habits, and brand equity alignment.


Category Managers: Retail professionals responsible for a specific product category, aiming to maximize revenue.
Key Decisions:
Aisle placement for the product(s).
Placement within the aisle (e.g., shelf height, proximity to other products).
Tools & Strategies:
Planogram: Visual representation of product placement on shelves, optimizing shelf space for sales.
Special Placement: End-caps, shipper/POS displays, check-out placement, shelf talkers, entry aisle displays, and promotion zones.
Placement Fees:
Display fees: Paid to retailers for premium placement.
Pay-to-stay fees: Paid to maintain shelf presence.
Slotting fees: Paid for introducing a new product to a retailer's shelves.


Omnichannel Retailing: Integrating online and offline channels to create a seamless customer experience.
Channel Integration: Combining different channel structures to reach a wider audience and create synergies.
Platform Models: New business models like sharing economy platforms (e.g., Uber, Airbnb) create new opportunities for reaching customers.


Definition: The use of communication to foster customer engagement behaviors such as buying behavior, word-of-mouth, etc.
Key Elements:
Market (target audience)
Mission (communication goals)
Message (content, structure, source)
Media (channels)
Money (budget)
Measurement (success metrics)


Target Audience: Defining the specific customer segments the communication aims to reach.
Consumer Profiling: Understanding the target audience's demographics, psychographics, behaviors, and media consumption habits.


Communication Goals:
Attract new customers
Encourage trials
Encourage brand switching
Remind customers
Stimulate extra sales
Enhance brand loyalty
Win-back lost customers
Showcase new product/service use
Create a brand image
Highlight brand position
Lower the cost of sales
Reduce cognitive dissonance
Goal Statement:
Identifies the target audience
Defines the desired impact on the audience
Sets a timeline for achieving the impact
Specifies the degree of change required
Product Life Cycle Stage: The communication mission will vary depending on whether the product is in the introduction, growth, maturity, or decline stage.


AIDA Model: Effective messages should follow the Attention, Interest, Desire, and Action framework.
Key Decisions:
Message Content: What to communicate (aligned with the value proposition, positioning statement, and USP).
Message Structure & Format: How to structure and format the message (informational vs. transformational appeal, use of humor, sex appeal, fear appeal).
Message Source: Who delivers the message (expert, celebrity, influencer, typical consumer).


Media Trifecta:
Paid Media: Brand pays for media space (e.g., advertising).
Owned Media: Brand controls the channels (e.g., website, social media).
Earned Media: Third-party coverage generated organically (e.g., user-generated content, reviews).
Channel Selection:
Consider target audience media consumption.
Align media choices with campaign goals.
Evaluate affordability and cost-effectiveness of each channel.
Digital Advertising: A rapidly growing segment of the advertising market, encompassing search advertising, social media advertising, banner advertising, video advertising, and classifieds.


Budgeting Approaches:
Fixed percentage of sales: Allocating a set percentage of sales revenue to advertising.
Task-based: Defining budget based on the required marketing tasks.
Residual-based: Allocating remaining funds to advertising after other expenses.
Same level as competitors: Benchmarking advertising spend against industry competitors.


Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): Metrics used to assess the effectiveness of the campaign. Examples:
Impressions
Gross Rating Points (GRPs)
Cost per Thousand Impressions (CPM)
Net Reach
Average Frequency
Effective Reach
Clickthrough Rate (CTR)
Cost per Click (CPC)

item.title: PP2
item.description:
#sponsored


Table of contents
Key Concepts
Place
Definition
Delivery Model
Channel Structures
Factors Impacting Channel Length
Retail Partner Selection
Retail Formats
Producer Considerations
Retailer Considerations
Potential Conflicts
In-store Place Decisions
Planogram
Special Places in Store
New Platforms
Omnichannel Retailing
Platform Models
Promotion
Definition
Integrated Marketing Communications Plan (6-M Model)
Market
Mission
Message
Key Decisions for Message Design
Influencer Marketing
Media
Money
Measurement
Conclusion


Place
Delivery model
Retail partner selection
In-store place decisions
New platforms
Promotion
6-M model
Designing messages
Influencer marketing
Media channels


Place refers to the process of moving products from the producer to the intended user, involving various parties both online and offline. It encompasses the logistics of distribution, retail locations, and the overall physical or virtual marketplace where transactions occur.

The delivery model outlines how products move from the producer to the consumer. Different models exist depending on the industry, target market, and product type.


Level 0 (Direct Distribution):
Producer -> Customers
Example: A local farmer selling produce directly to consumers at a farmer's market.
Level 1 (Retailer Intermediary):
Producer -> Retailer -> Customers
Example: Clothing brands selling through department stores.
Level 2 (Wholesaler and Retailer):
Producer -> Wholesaler/Distributor -> Retailer -> Customers
Example: A beverage company selling to a wholesaler, who then distributes to local grocery stores.
Level 3 (Agent/Broker, Wholesaler, and Retailer):
Producer -> Agent/Broker -> Wholesaler/Distributor -> Retailer -> Customers
Example: Imported goods often go through several intermediaries before reaching the consumer.

Several factors influence the choice of a short or long channel structure:

Market Factors:
Business Users vs. Consumers: Business users may prefer direct channels for bulk purchasing, while consumers may use retail intermediaries.
Geographic Concentration: Products targeting geographically concentrated markets may use direct channels, whereas diverse markets may require longer channels.
Technical Knowledge and Servicing: Products needing extensive technical knowledge and regular servicing might use shorter channels to ensure quality service.
Order Size: Larger orders may justify direct channels, while smaller, frequent orders may benefit from intermediary use.
Product Factors:
Perishability: Perishable goods often use shorter channels to minimize spoilage.
Complexity: Complex products may require direct channels to provide better customer support.
Standardization: Standardized products can leverage longer channels due to lower service requirements.
Cost: Expensive items might use shorter channels to ensure better control over sales and service quality.
Producer Factors:
Resources: Producers with adequate resources might manage distribution functions internally, preferring shorter channels.
Product Line: A broad product line might necessitate a shorter channel to streamline distribution.
Channel Control: Companies seeking greater control over their product distribution prefer shorter channels.
Competitive Factors:
Intermediary Performance: Satisfaction with intermediaries' performance can influence channel length. Poor performance may lead to shorter channels.
Market Saturation: In saturated markets, shorter channels may help in distinguishing the brand and providing better customer service.

Retail partner selection involves choosing the appropriate retail outlets to distribute products. This decision impacts the product's availability, positioning, and overall market presence.

Various retail formats can be used depending on the product and target market:

Specialty Stores: Focus on a specific product category (e.g., electronics, fashion).
Department Stores: Offer a wide range of products across multiple categories.
Supermarkets: Primarily sell food and household items.
Convenience Stores: Small stores offering a limited range of products, often with extended hours.
Discount Stores: Offer products at lower prices.
Off-Price Retailers: Sell branded goods at reduced prices.
Superstores/Hypermarkets: Large stores combining a supermarket and a department store.
Online Retailers/Marketplaces: E-commerce platforms allowing customers to buy products online.

When selecting retail partners, producers consider several factors:

Product Availability: Ensuring the product is available where target customers shop.
Distribution Intensity: Choosing between intensive (wide availability), selective (limited outlets), or exclusive (few outlets) distribution.
In-Store Positioning: Securing optimal shelf space and positioning within the store.
Promotional Ability: Evaluating the retailer's ability to conduct effective promotions.
Cost-Effectiveness: Balancing the cost of distribution with expected sales volume.
Customer Considerations: Understanding where customers prefer to shop and what factors influence their store choice.

Retailers also evaluate potential producers based on:

Investment Requirements: The level of investment needed to support the product introduction and maintain the producer relationship.
Revenue Potential: Expected profitability, conversion rates, and average customer basket size.
Assortment Competition: How the new product fits with existing products and its impact on private labels.

Conflicts may arise between producers and retailers, impacting the overall distribution strategy:

Horizontal Conflict: Occurs between firms at the same level (e.g., two retailers competing).
Vertical Conflict: Occurs between firms at different levels (e.g., producer vs. retailer).
Channel Power: The ability of one channel member to influence others, often based on expertise, rewards, sanctions, or business size.

In-store place decisions focus on product placement within the retail environment, typically managed by category managers.

A planogram is a visual representation that details where products should be placed on shelves and how many facings they should have. It is used by retailers and suppliers to optimize shelf space and increase sales.

Special product placements can enhance visibility and sales:

End-cap Displays: Located at the end of aisles to attract attention.
Check-out Placement: Positioned near the register for impulse purchases.
Entry Aisle Promotion Zones: Placed at the store entrance to catch customers' attention as they enter.

New platforms and technologies are transforming traditional retail and distribution models.

Omnichannel retailing integrates multiple channels (online and offline) to enhance customer experience and sales, creating synergies rather than cannibalizing channels.

Different platform models offer various approaches to distribution and sales:

Marketer-Provided Resources: Resources provided by the marketer or producer.
Peer-to-Peer-Provided Resources: Resources shared or provided by peers or users.
Access-Based: Models where customers access products without ownership.
Transfer of Ownership: Traditional models where ownership is transferred upon purchase.


Promotion involves using communication to foster customer engagement behaviors such as buying or word-of-mouth. It includes a wide range of activities aimed at influencing the target audience's perceptions and actions.

The 6-M model outlines a comprehensive approach to planning and executing promotional campaigns:

Market: Identify and select target segments.
Mission: Define communication goals and objectives.
Message: Develop the content, structure, and source of the message.
Media: Choose appropriate media channels.
Money: Determine the budget.
Measurement: Evaluate the campaign’s success with specific KPIs.

Effective communication strategies depend on understanding the target audience. Identifying and selecting customer segments is crucial for tailoring messages and choosing the right media.

The mission defines the specific goals of the communication campaign. These goals can include:

Attracting new customers
Winning back lost customers
Encouraging trials
Showcasing product/service use
Encouraging brand switching
Creating a brand image
Reminding customers
Highlighting brand position
Stimulating extra sales
Lowering the cost of sales
Enhancing brand loyalty
Reducing cognitive dissonance

A well-designed message is critical for effective communication. It should align with the brand's value proposition and positioning statement.


Message Content: What message to deliver based on communication goals.
Message Structure & Format: How to structure and format the message to ensure clarity and engagement.
Message Source: Who delivers the message (e.g., experts, celebrities, influencers, typical consumers).

Influencers can significantly boost brand awareness, product consideration, and engagement. Key considerations for influencer marketing include:

Relevance: Fit with the product and audience.
Reach: The number of people the influencer can reach.
Resonance: The level of engagement the influencer can create.
Trust: How well the influencer is perceived by their audience.
Sponsorship Saturation: Frequency of the influencer's promotions and alignment with other sponsored products.
Budget: Cost of bringing the influencer on board.
Creative Freedom: Degree of creative control given to the influencer.
Transparency: Openness about being sponsored (#ad, #sponsored).

Selecting the right media channels is crucial for reaching the target audience effectively. The media trifecta includes:

Paid Media: Advertising through paid channels (e.g., TV ads, social media ads).
Owned Media: Channels controlled by the brand (e.g., website, social media pages).
Earned Media: Publicity gained through word-of-mouth or organic mentions (e.g., reviews, shares).

Determining the budget involves balancing various approaches:

Fixed Percentage of Sales: Budget based on a percentage of sales revenue.
Task-Based: Budget based on specific tasks or objectives the advertising needs to accomplish.
Residual-Based: Budget based on what remains after other expenses.
Competitive Parity: Budget based on competitors' spending levels.

Evaluating the success of a promotional campaign requires specific metrics:

Impressions: Number of times an ad is viewed.
Gross Rating Points (GRPs): Measure of impressions relative to the audience size.
Cost per Thousand Impressions (CPM): Cost-effectiveness of generating impressions.
Net Reach: Number of unique individuals who receive an ad.
Average Frequency: Average number of times an individual sees an ad.
Effective Reach: Reach among individuals exposed to the ad enough times to be influenced.
Clickthrough Rate (CTR): Effectiveness of web ads based on the number of clicks.
Cost per Click (CPC): Cost-effectiveness of generating clicks.

Effective marketing management in terms of place and promotion requires strategic planning and execution. By understanding and leveraging various models and techniques, businesses can optimize product distribution and promotional efforts to enhance customer engagement and drive sales.
